stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a metropolitan ED reviewing a non-ambulant 8-month-old boy brought in by his mother and her current partner. The history is that he 'rolled off the couch' onto a carpeted floor 4 hours ago. On examination you find a 2 cm fluctuant boggy swelling over the right parietal scalp, three small ovoid bruises over the left upper arm in a grip distribution, a torn upper labial frenulum, and a small healing burn on the dorsum of the left hand. He is interactive but quiet; his fontanelle is soft. The partner cannot describe the incident in detail. The mother appears anxious. The infant has not previously been seen at this hospital and the family is not known to child protection services.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 38, SpO2 99% in air, T 36.8 degrees C, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Right parietal boggy swelling 2 cm, three ovoid bruises left upper arm with grip pattern, torn upper labial frenulum, healing dorsal hand burn 1 cm, no spinal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Bedside neurological examination intact; no investigations performed at this stage

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit him for a non-contrast CT brain, a skeletal survey within 24 hours, dilated fundoscopy by ophthalmology, paediatric child protection team consultation, and notify the statutory child protection agency before discharge
options.rationale: Multiple sentinel injuries in a non-ambulant infant (torn frenulum, patterned bruising, scalp haematoma, healing burn) mandate a structured non-accidental injury workup and statutory child protection notification under all Australian mandatory reporting frameworks. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Order a non-contrast CT brain only and discharge home with the mother once the scan is reported as normal, deferring the skeletal survey and child protection consultation to outpatient paediatric follow-up next week
options.rationale: A normal CT brain does not exclude abusive injury or skeletal fractures; skeletal survey, fundoscopy and inpatient child protection team review are part of the recognised package of investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Arrange social work involvement and a community child health nurse home visit within 7 days, and ask the mother to bring him back to ED only if he develops new bruises or any signs of head injury such as vomiting
options.rationale: Community escalation alone is insufficient when sentinel injuries are present; the threshold for inpatient workup and statutory notification is met and cannot be delegated to outpatient services. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Discharge him home with the mother after a discussion about home safety, provide written safety advice on supervised tummy time off raised surfaces, and arrange GP review within a week to recheck the scalp haematoma and the healing burn
options.rationale: Discharge without statutory notification or imaging in a non-ambulant infant with multiple sentinel injuries breaches mandatory reporting obligations and risks repeat injury including life-threatening abusive head trauma. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Take a more detailed history from the mother and partner separately and re-examine in 4 hours; if the explanation reconciles with the injuries, discharge with GP follow-up, otherwise consider further investigation later
options.rationale: Sentinel injuries in a non-ambulant infant require workup and notification regardless of how plausibly the history is rephrased; pre-judging plausibility before completing investigation and notification is unsafe practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

explanation.summary: Sentinel injuries in a non-ambulant infant - bruising in patterned distributions, torn frenulum, scalp haematoma, burns - have a high specificity for inflicted injury. The RCH and statewide child protection pathways require admission for structured workup (CT brain, skeletal survey within 24 hours, dilated fundoscopy, coagulation screen) alongside specialist consultation and statutory notification. Mandatory reporting obligations apply to all Australian medical practitioners; you do not need certainty, only a reasonable suspicion. Failing to act risks fatal recurrence.
key_points: Bruising in a non-ambulant infant is highly suspicious for inflicted injury until proven otherwise
Recognised sentinel injuries: torn frenulum, ear bruising, patterned bruises, burns, scalp haematoma in non-ambulant infant
Workup: CT brain, skeletal survey at 0 and at 11 to 14 days, fundoscopy, coagulation, LFTs, amylase/lipase
Mandatory reporting in all Australian jurisdictions; reasonable suspicion is the threshold, not proof
explanation.pearls: Bruises in those who do not cruise are a red flag - in a non-ambulant infant, a single unexplained bruise warrants the full sentinel injury workup, and any combination as here mandates inpatient admission and statutory notification before discharge.

sources.label: AIFS: Mandatory reporting of child abuse and neglect

subtopic_detail: Mycoplasma pneumoniae atypical pneumonia in school-aged child
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a previously well 9-year-old girl with 10 days of dry cough that has progressed to a paroxysmal cough productive of clear sputum and 4 days of fever to 38.5 degrees C, gradually worsening despite oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily prescribed by her GP on day 4. She has bilateral mild widespread crackles and scattered fine wheeze, no focal consolidation on examination, and is well perfused, alert and tolerating oral fluids. Chest x-ray shows bilateral patchy peribronchial infiltrates without lobar consolidation or pleural effusion. Two of her classmates have had similar prolonged coughs recently and her dry cough has continued to disrupt her sleep at night.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100, RR 24, SpO2 96% in air, T 38.4 degrees C, BP 102/64, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mild bilateral crackles, scattered fine wheeze, no focal consolidation, no respiratory distress, no extrapulmonary rash, oropharynx clear
data_table.Investigations: CXR: bilateral patchy peribronchial infiltrates, no lobar consolidation, no effusion; WCC 9.5 x10^9/L, CRP 28 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in antimicrobial management?

options.text: Cease the amoxicillin and commence oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg on day 1 followed by 5 mg/kg daily for a further 4 days, with safety-net advice about return precautions and a planned GP review at 48 to 72 hours
options.rationale: The clinical picture (prolonged dry cough, bilateral interstitial infiltrates, lack of response to amoxicillin in a school-aged child) fits Mycoplasma pneumoniae; oral azithromycin is recommended first-line atypical cover in Australian guidance. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia paediatric)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia paediatric

options.text: Add oral doxycycline 2 mg/kg twice daily for 10 days in addition to her current amoxicillin to broaden cover to atypical pathogens while preserving pneumococcal cover until the cough resolves clinically
options.rationale: Doxycycline is avoided under 8 years in Australia outside specific scenarios (and azithromycin is the standard atypical cover in this age group); dual therapy with amoxicillin is unnecessary when the picture clearly fits atypical pneumonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia paediatric)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia paediatric

options.text: Stop antibiotics altogether and arrange a chest CT angiogram with intravenous contrast to exclude pulmonary embolism, foreign body or interstitial lung disease as the explanation for the persistent cough
options.rationale: There is a coherent infective explanation with classmate clustering, atypical clinical pattern and a typical chest x-ray; advanced imaging is not indicated as the next investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

options.text: Increase the oral amoxicillin to 45 mg/kg three times daily for a further 7 days to extend the bacterial cover for partially treated typical pneumococcal pneumonia given her improvement plateau and the recent classmate outbreaks
options.rationale: Amoxicillin lacks activity against Mycoplasma pneumoniae and dose escalation will not cover the most likely pathogen in this age and clinical pattern; escalating beta-lactam dosing is the wrong axis of change here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia paediatric)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia paediatric

options.text: Admit her for intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg six-hourly for at least 48 hours and arrange a CT chest if she has not defervesced clinically by 48 hours into the intravenous treatment course
options.rationale: She is well-perfused, normoxic and tolerating oral fluids; she does not meet admission criteria and intravenous beta-lactam does not cover Mycoplasma pneumoniae. (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

explanation.summary: Mycoplasma pneumoniae becomes the dominant pneumonia pathogen in school-aged children and adolescents and presents with a protracted dry cough, low-grade fever, bilateral interstitial changes on chest x-ray, and characteristically poor response to beta-lactam antibiotics. First-line treatment in Australia is oral azithromycin (10 mg/kg day 1, then 5 mg/kg daily for 4 days). Doxycycline is generally avoided under 8 years; intravenous therapy or admission are reserved for hypoxia, severe respiratory distress, dehydration, complications or failure of oral therapy.
key_points: Mycoplasma pneumoniae predominates in pneumonia from school-age onward
Classic features: prolonged dry cough, low-grade fever, bilateral interstitial infiltrates, classmate clustering
First-line: azithromycin 10 mg/kg day 1 then 5 mg/kg daily for 4 days (5-day total course)
Doxycycline reserved for over-8s; amoxicillin lacks Mycoplasma activity and should not be escalated for atypical pneumonia
explanation.pearls: If a school-aged child fails amoxicillin at 48 to 72 hours with bilateral interstitial changes and ongoing dry cough, switch to azithromycin first rather than escalating beta-lactam dose or jumping to intravenous therapy.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia paediatric

subtopic_detail: Polycystic ovary syndrome diagnosis using Rotterdam criteria
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 24-year-old nulligravid woman with 18 months of irregular cycles ranging from 45 to 90 days, troublesome facial and lower-abdominal hirsutism that she shaves twice weekly, and adult-onset acne. She is not on hormonal contraception, has had no abnormal cervical screening, and has a BMI of 31 with central distribution. She is not trying to conceive. Targeted assessment shows total testosterone in the upper-normal range with a low SHBG and a normal free androgen index calculation aligned with that. TSH, prolactin, early-morning 17-hydroxyprogesterone, day-21 progesterone and HbA1c are normal; transvaginal ultrasound shows 14 follicles measuring 2 to 9 mm in one ovary and increased ovarian volume on the other.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76, HR 78, BMI 31, waist circumference 92 cm
data_table.Examination: Modified Ferriman-Gallwey score 10, mild acanthosis nigricans at nape, mild adult acne on jawline, no Cushingoid features
data_table.Investigations: Total testosterone upper-normal with low SHBG; TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP, day-21 progesterone, HbA1c normal; TVUS 14 follicles 2-9 mm one ovary, increased ovarian volume contralaterally

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer her to a reproductive endocrinology clinic for letrozole ovulation induction immediately and counsel her about ovarian drilling as a second-line surgical option if pharmacotherapy fails to restore ovulation
options.rationale: Letrozole ovulation induction is reserved for women trying to conceive; she is not planning pregnancy and current management priorities are menstrual cycle control, hyperandrogenism and metabolic risk. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS 2023)

options.text: Defer the diagnosis until she has undergone a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test, a 24-hour urinary free cortisol, plasma metanephrines, and a fasting lipid panel to first exclude any alternative endocrine cause for her symptoms
options.rationale: Cushing's, phaeochromocytoma and dyslipidaemia are not routine exclusions for PCOS diagnosis; she has already had appropriate exclusion of thyroid, prolactin, CAH and pregnancy-related causes. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS 2023)

options.text: Diagnose polycystic ovary syndrome based on her oligomenorrhoea, clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism and polycystic ovarian morphology, and commence a combined oral contraceptive pill with metformin as initial therapy
options.rationale: She meets all three Rotterdam criteria, secondary causes have been excluded, and the international evidence-based guideline supports COCP for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism with metformin as adjunct for metabolic features. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS 2023)

options.text: Commence cyproterone acetate as monotherapy at a dose of 50 mg daily for 21 days each cycle to control her hirsutism and acne and reassess after a 6-month treatment trial before considering any further interventions
options.rationale: Cyproterone monotherapy is not first-line: COCP plus anti-androgen is reserved for inadequate response after 6 months of COCP, and contraception is mandatory due to teratogenicity of anti-androgens in pregnancy. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS 2023)

options.text: Arrange a repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 3 months and a serum anti-Mullerian hormone level before any diagnosis is formalised, since polycystic ovarian morphology alone is not adequate without additional confirmatory imaging
options.rationale: Repeat imaging adds no value when the Rotterdam criteria are clearly met; AMH is supportive but is not required to confirm a clinically secure diagnosis. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS 2023)

explanation.summary: Rotterdam criteria require two of three: oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound, after exclusion of mimics (thyroid, prolactin, CAH). When all three are met as here, the diagnosis is secure. Initial management for a woman not trying to conceive centres on COCP for cycle regulation and androgen control, metformin for metabolic features, and lifestyle intervention. Letrozole is for women seeking pregnancy; cyproterone monotherapy is second-line for refractory hirsutism. AMH and OGTT add little to a clinically clear case.
key_points: Rotterdam: 2 of 3 - oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, PCO morphology; exclude thyroid, prolactin, CAH
First-line for non-conceiving woman: combined oral contraceptive plus lifestyle; metformin for metabolic features
Letrozole is first-line for ovulation induction when fertility is the goal, not for general PCOS treatment
Cyproterone or spironolactone is added if 6 months of COCP plus lifestyle does not control hirsutism
explanation.pearls: When Rotterdam criteria are met and CAH/thyroid/prolactin are normal, the diagnosis is made: do not delay treatment waiting for AMH or repeat ultrasound, and pick management based on whether the woman is currently trying to conceive.

stem: You are the early pregnancy assessment service registrar reviewing a 28-year-old gravida 2 para 1 woman at 9+2 weeks gestation by certain dates who has had two episodes of moderate bright red painless vaginal bleeding without clots over the last 8 hours. She is haemodynamically stable; her cervix is closed on speculum examination with a small amount of fresh blood at the os; transvaginal ultrasound shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy with crown-rump length consistent with dates, fetal heart of 162 beats per minute, and a small subchorionic haematoma. Her blood group is O Rhesus D negative; she has no anti-D antibodies on routine antibody screen and her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated and resulted in a vaginal birth at term with appropriate postpartum anti-D administration.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 84, SpO2 99% in air, T 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, speculum: closed cervix with fresh blood at os, no products, no adnexal mass on bimanual
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L; group and antibody screen: O Rh D negative, antibody screen negative; beta-hCG 56,000 IU/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding anti-D immunoglobulin?

options.text: Refer her urgently to maternal fetal medicine for cordocentesis to determine fetal Rhesus D status before deciding whether anti-D is needed for this episode of antenatal sensitising vaginal bleeding event today
options.rationale: Invasive fetal Rh typing is not indicated for antenatal anti-D decisions; non-invasive prenatal Rh-D genotyping from maternal plasma is increasingly available but not part of the standard sensitising-event response. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer 625 IU of intramuscular anti-D immunoglobulin now as the standard antenatal sensitising-event dose and arrange a repeat antibody screen in 6 weeks to confirm there has been no sensitisation in this pregnancy
options.rationale: The 625 IU dose is for sensitising events at or after 13 weeks and for routine 28- and 34-week antenatal prophylaxis; it is not recommended in a viable first-trimester pregnancy with threatened miscarriage. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Defer the anti-D decision until a fetomaternal haemorrhage flow cytometry result is available within 24 hours and dose her anti-D based on the calculated volume of fetal red cells that have crossed the placenta today
options.rationale: Quantitative fetomaternal haemorrhage testing is not used to guide first-trimester anti-D decisions; anti-D in this gestation is determined by clinical event type, not by quantified bleed volume. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Reassure her, document the threatened miscarriage with confirmed viability on ultrasound, and discharge with safety-net advice without administering anti-D immunoglobulin at this gestation given the clinical picture today
options.rationale: Australian guidance does not recommend routine anti-D prophylaxis for threatened miscarriage with a viable intrauterine pregnancy under 12 weeks; sensitising events at this gestation do not produce significant fetomaternal haemorrhage. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer 250 IU of intramuscular anti-D immunoglobulin now and repeat the dose if any further bleeding occurs in the next 72 hours, and arrange a Kleihauer-Betke test before each subsequent anti-D administration
options.rationale: Anti-D is not required for threatened miscarriage with a viable intrauterine pregnancy before 12 weeks; routine Kleihauer testing in the first trimester is also not recommended in Australian guidance. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: Australian National Blood Authority guidance separates anti-D prophylaxis indications by gestation. Before 12 weeks, anti-D is required for sensitising events that disrupt the choriodecidual interface (miscarriage, termination, ectopic, chorionic villus sampling) but is NOT required for threatened miscarriage with a viable intrauterine pregnancy. From 13 weeks, all sensitising events trigger 625 IU anti-D, and routine 625 IU doses are given at 28 and 34 weeks regardless. Quantitative fetomaternal haemorrhage testing applies from 20 weeks. Invasive fetal genotyping is not routinely used.
key_points: Threatened miscarriage with viable IUP under 12 weeks: anti-D NOT recommended
Miscarriage, termination, ectopic, CVS under 12 weeks: 250 IU anti-D within 72 hours
Sensitising event 13 weeks or beyond: 625 IU anti-D within 72 hours
Routine antenatal: 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks for Rh D negative women without preformed anti-D
explanation.pearls: First-trimester anti-D decisions hinge on event type, not bleed volume: a viable pregnancy with threatened miscarriage does not need anti-D, but a miscarriage, ectopic or termination under 12 weeks needs 250 IU.

sources.label: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

stem: You are the sexual health clinic registrar consulting with a 32-year-old para 2 woman requesting a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system for both contraception and management of heavy menstrual bleeding. She has no significant past medical history; her cervical screening is current and normal; she has one stable male partner of 5 years and uses condoms intermittently. She had unprotected intercourse 5 days ago. Her last menstrual period began 16 days ago. Bimanual pelvic examination is unremarkable. As part of pre-insertion workup, a self-collected vulvovaginal nucleic acid amplification test returns positive for Chlamydia trachomatis with the speciation of the request now confirmed by the on-site sexual health laboratory.

data_table.Examination: External genitalia normal, speculum: scant clear discharge, no cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass on bimanual examination
data_table.Investigations: Vulvovaginal NAAT: Chlamydia trachomatis positive; gonorrhoea NAAT negative; cervical screening current

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed with same-day insertion of the levonorgestrel intrauterine system and prescribe a single oral azithromycin 1 g dose at the time of the procedure to cover the recently detected chlamydia infection during the IUD insertion
options.rationale: Inserting an IUD through a chlamydia-infected cervix substantially increases the risk of ascending pelvic infection; defer insertion until treatment is complete, and Australian first-line for genital chlamydia is doxycycline not azithromycin. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Insert the intrauterine system today and arrange a test-of-cure NAAT for chlamydia 4 weeks after the procedure to confirm clearance, without treating with oral antibiotics either before or after the insertion of the device
options.rationale: Untreated active chlamydia at insertion markedly raises PID risk; routine test-of-cure is not recommended for uncomplicated chlamydia in Australia but a test-of-reinfection at 3 months is. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Defer insertion and prescribe oral azithromycin 1 g single dose for her and her partner now, then proceed with insertion at 1 week post treatment without rechecking for resolution of the chlamydia infection prior to insertion
options.rationale: Doxycycline is preferred over azithromycin for genital chlamydia in Australia due to higher cure rates, and partner notification through contact tracing rather than empirical co-prescription is the recommended approach. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Insert a copper intrauterine device today instead of the hormonal device since the copper device also provides emergency contraception coverage for the unprotected intercourse 5 days ago in this clinical context
options.rationale: Active genital chlamydia is a contraindication to insertion of any IUD (copper or hormonal); switching device type does not address the underlying infection-related ascending risk. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Defer the intrauterine system insertion, prescribe oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days for her and contact tracing for her partner, and rebook her for the procedure once she has completed treatment and is asymptomatic
options.rationale: Active chlamydia infection is a temporary contraindication to IUD insertion under MEC category 4 for current cervicitis or PID; treat first, perform contact tracing, then schedule insertion once treatment is complete. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Active genital chlamydia is a temporary contraindication to IUD insertion because instrumentation through an infected cervix can precipitate pelvic inflammatory disease. The correct sequence is treat (doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days), partner notification and contact tracing, then insertion once treatment is complete. Australian guidance prefers doxycycline over single-dose azithromycin for genital chlamydia. Routine test of cure is not recommended for uncomplicated cases, but a test of reinfection at 3 months is standard. Copper IUD as emergency contraception requires the same infection-free status.
key_points: Active chlamydia/gonorrhoea/PID is MEC category 4 for IUD insertion (temporary)
First-line chlamydia: oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days
Partner notification and contact tracing for partners in the past 6 months
Test of reinfection at 3 months for chlamydia; no routine test of cure
explanation.pearls: Always swab before insertion: an asymptomatic positive NAAT changes management - treat, trace, then reschedule the IUD, never insert through an active infection regardless of how stable her partnership appears.

sources.label: Family Planning NSW: IUD insertion clinical guidance
